speaker: doctor
text: Good morning. Let's start with your vitals — your temperature today is 36.7 °C, which is within normal range.

speaker: doctor
text: You're currently on Amlodipine 10mg once daily for blood pressure control. How have you been tolerating it?

speaker: patient
text: Pretty well, no dizziness or swelling. My energy’s been okay, though I’ve felt a bit more tired lately.

speaker: doctor
text: Your hemoglobin came back at 99 g/L on today’s lab draw — that’s mildly low and could contribute to fatigue.

speaker: patient
text: Oh, really? I didn’t know that could be the cause. I’ve also had some occasional blurry vision in the past month.

speaker: doctor
text: Your diastolic blood pressure is 78 mmHg — solidly in target range.

speaker: doctor
text: Based on your labs, symptoms, and history, we’re confirming a diagnosis of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus.

speaker: doctor
text: Your systolic blood pressure is 114 mmHg — excellent control overall.

speaker: patient
text: That’s reassuring. But my fasting glucose readings at home have been running between 150–180 mg/dL lately.

speaker: doctor
text: That aligns with the diabetes diagnosis. We’ll need to address both glycemic control and your anemia. Any history of gastrointestinal issues or heavy periods?

speaker: patient
text: No GI bleeding, but I did have menorrhagia a few years ago — though it’s been stable since my uterine ablation.

speaker: doctor
text: Understood. Let’s hold off on iron supplementation for now until we review your ferritin and reticulocyte count next week.

speaker: doctor
text: Actually, your repeat hemoglobin just came in at 129 g/L — quite a jump from the earlier 99 g/L. There may have been a lab mix-up or sample issue.

speaker: patient
text: That’s strange — I only gave one blood sample. Could the first result have been drawn during my morning coffee? I hadn’t fasted then.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s possible — your most recent temperature reading is 36.0 °C, slightly lower than the 36.7 °C we recorded earlier. Were you feeling chilly when that was taken?

speaker: patient
text: Yes, the exam room was cold, and I’d just come in from outside. But my thermometer at home reads 36.7 °C — same as your first measurement.

speaker: doctor
text: Noted — your diastolic pressure is now 84 mmHg, up modestly from the 78 mmHg we saw earlier. Still well-controlled, but let’s monitor trends.

speaker: patient
text: Right — that 36.7 °C reading matches what I saw this morning, so I think that’s the more accurate one.

speaker: doctor
text: Given your elevated fasting glucose and confirmed Type 2 Diabetes, we’ll initiate pharmacologic therapy. I’m going to switch you to Metformin 1000mg twice daily, starting tomorrow.

speaker: patient
text: Okay — and just to confirm, my diastolic pressure is still 78 mmHg, like it was earlier, not the 84 we saw just now.
